title: Rescued pocket-sized owl turning heads in Mass.
author: Abby Patkin
bullets: A tiny saw-whet owl was rescued after being found injured in a New England driveway.
The owl, unable to fly, is receiving medical care at Cape Ann Wildlife for two broken bones.
Newhouse Wildlife Rescue shared the owl's story and fundraiser for Cape Ann, aiding their work.

title: Massachusetts joins insurers cutting coverage for popular weight-loss drugs
author: Beth Treffeisen
bullets: Massachusetts Group Insurance Commission voted 10-7 to end GLP-1 drug coverage for weight loss due to high costs.
Approximately 22,000 state employees use GLP-1 medications, costing the state $46 million annually.
Controversial decision aims to save $100 million and reduce premium growth to 7.5% amid budget pressures.

title: After Columbia incident, Healey and Campbell remind schools that ICE needs a judicial warrant to enter dorms
author: Ross Cristantiello
bullets: Massachusetts officials issued guidance reminding schools that ICE must have a judicial warrant to enter dorm buildings.
The guidance follows federal agents entering a Columbia building under false pretenses to reach a student's apartment.
Governor Healey signed an order preventing ICE arrests in non-public state buildings and staging operations on state property.

title: Lawsuit challenges National Park Service over content removal, including at a Mass. site
author: Samantha Genzer
bullets: Coalition files lawsuit in Boston challenging Trump's order on history content in National Parks.
Lowell National Historical Park stopped showing films on labor history to comply with Interior order.
Massachusetts Sen. Ed Markey criticizes content removal, calls lawsuit against Trump's order patriotic.

title: Mass. police dispatcher injured in Iran conflict
author: Abby Patkin
bullets: A Sudbury police dispatcher serving with the U.S. Navy Reserve was slightly injured in missile strikes near Tehran, Iran.
The 22-year-old dispatcher was at a base in Dubai when Iranian missiles struck within 50 yards, causing minor injuries.
The U.S.-Israeli strikes on Iran led to the death of Iranian Supreme Leader Ayatollah Khamenei and condemnation from Mass. lawmakers.

title: What Baylor Scheierman said about his fractured thumb
author: Khari A. Thompson
bullets: Baylor Scheierman played 32 minutes and scored 12 points against the 76ers despite a fractured thumb.
The thumb injury occurred during a collision with Nets' Egor Demin, causing temporary locking, but he continued playing.
Scheierman wore a splint during the Sunday game and plans to keep playing as injury is being evaluated.

title: ‘It feels like home’: Chinatown celebrates the Year of the Horse
author: Samantha Genzer
bullets: Chinatown's annual Lunar New Year parade featured about 10 lion dance troupes celebrating the Year of the Horse.
The event was organized by Chinatown Main Street and the Chinese Consolidated Benevolent Association of New England.
Gund Kwok, an all-women dance troupe, performed a lion dance and a horse-themed routine to 'Beat It'.

title: Boston 25 News promotes Shiri Spear to chief meteorologist
author: Heather Alterisio
bullets: Shiri Spear promoted to chief meteorologist at Boston 25 News, overseeing all platform weather coverage and leading the expert team.
Spear succeeds Kevin Lemanowicz, who stepped down after 30 years, and continues as anchor on Boston 25 Morning News.
Spear holds degrees in mathematics education and atmospheric science, and is an American Meteorological Society certified broadcast meteorologist.

title: R.I. prosecutor who told police ‘I’m an AG’ during arrest is back at work
author: Abby Patkin
bullets: Devon Flanagan, a Rhode Island prosecutor, returned to work after an unpaid leave post misdemeanor trespassing charge.
Flanagan reassigned from Criminal Division to Providence County Superior Court following a Newport arrest incident.
Attorney General Peter Neronha criticized Flanagan's behavior during arrest, urging her to reflect on her actions.

title: Should pajamas be banned at Logan Airport? Here’s what readers said.
author: Kristi Palma
bullets: 48% of Boston.com readers support a pajama ban at Logan Airport, 42% oppose, and 11% say it depends on style.
Discussions on pajama bans followed Tampa Airport's joking suggestion, sparking nationwide conversation about travel attire.
Some readers cite comfort and personal rights for wearing pajamas; others emphasize respect and societal standards.
